questions.question: Define rationality in the context of intelligent agents. Explain how a rational agent differs from an omniscient agent, and describe the four dimensions along which task environments can be characterized.
keyPoints: Rationality: performance measure, prior knowledge, actions, percept sequence
Rational agent: selects actions that maximize expected performance measure
Omniscient agent: knows actual outcome of all actions (theoretical only)
PEAS: Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors

questions.question: Classify the following environments according to all relevant properties: (a) Automated taxi driving system, (b) Chess game, (c) Medical diagnosis system, (d) Internet shopping agent. Consider observability, determinism, episodicity, dynamism, discreteness, and number of agents.
keyPoints: Taxi driving: partially observable, stochastic, sequential, dynamic, continuous, multi-agent
Chess: fully observable, deterministic, sequential, static, discrete, multi-agent
Medical diagnosis: partially observable, stochastic, episodic, dynamic (patient changes), continuous, multi-agent
Internet shopping: fully observable, deterministic, episodic, static, discrete, multi-agent

questions.question: A hospital intensive care unit (ICU) needs an intelligent patient monitoring system. The system must continuously monitor vital signs (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation), detect anomalies, alert medical staff, and suggest appropriate interventions. Design an appropriate agent architecture for this task. Justify your choice by analyzing: (i) Why simpler reflex-based agents would be inadequate, (ii) How your chosen architecture handles uncertainty in medical data, (iii) The role of internal state representation, and (iv) How the system balances immediate response with long-term patient outcome optimization.
keyPoints: Recommend utility-based or goal-based agent architecture for handling multiple objectives (patient survival, comfort, resource optimization)
Model-based design required to maintain patient history and predict deterioration
Utility function needed to weigh trade-offs between aggressive intervention risks vs. benefits
Partial observability: sensors noisy, patient state not fully known; need probabilistic reasoning

questions.question: Compare and contrast the four main types of intelligent agents: Reflex, Model-Based, Goal-Based, and Utility-Based. For each type: (a) Explain the internal architecture and decision-making mechanism, (b) Identify the type of environment where it performs best, (c) Provide a real-world AI application example, and (d) Discuss its limitations. Finally, analyze a self-driving car scenario and explain which agent type(s) would be most appropriate for different subsystems (obstacle avoidance, route planning, passenger comfort optimization).
keyPoints: Reflex: condition-action rules, no internal state, suitable for fully observable, simple environments (e.g., thermostat control)
Model-based: maintains internal world state, handles partial observability, suitable for more complex environments (e.g., robotic navigation)
Goal-based: considers future consequences, planning, suitable for complex goal-oriented tasks (e.g., navigation systems)
Utility-based: maximizes utility function, handles trade-offs, suitable for optimization under uncertainty (e.g., autonomous vehicles)
